hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'homines' — 'hominum' with -um is genitive plural
hints.Accusative: Accusative plural would be 'homines' — 'hominum' is genitive
hints.Dative: Dative plural would be 'hominibus' — 'hominum' is genitive plural

hints.1st: 1st declension genitive plural ends in -arum — 'hominum' with -um is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension genitive plural ends in -orum — 'hominum' is 3rd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension genitive plural ends in -uum — 'hominum' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: While the form is the same, 'O' signals direct address — this is vocative
hints.Accusative: Accusative of neuter 'genus' is the same form, but 'O' marks it as vocative
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'genere' — 'genus' here is vocative with 'O'

hints.2nd: 2nd declension neuters end in -um — 'genus, generis' is 3rd declension neuter
hints.4th: 4th declension nouns end in -us/-u with different genitive — 'genus, generis' is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension nouns end in -es — 'genus' is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural would be 'animi' — 'animos' with -os is accusative plural
hints.Ablative: Ablative plural would be 'animis' — 'animos' is accusative
hints.Genitive: Genitive plural would be 'animorum' — 'animos' is accusative plural

hints.1st: 1st declension accusative plural ends in -as — 'animos' with -os is 2nd declension
hints.3rd: 3rd declension accusative plural ends in -es — 'animos' is 2nd declension
hints.4th: 4th declension accusative plural ends in -us — 'animos' with -os is 2nd declension

hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'amorem' — 'amor' is nominative, subject of 'regat'
hints.Vocative: 'amor' is not being addressed — it is the subject of the conditional clause

hints.to lead back: 'to lead back' is 'reducere' — 'regitur' comes from 'regere' (to rule)
hints.to ask: 'to ask' is 'rogare' — do not confuse with 'regere'
hints.to straighten: 'to straighten' is 'dirigere' — 'regere' means to rule or govern

hints.Present active indicative: Active would be 'regit' — 'regitur' with -tur is passive
hints.Present passive subjunctive: Passive subjunctive would be 'regatur' — 'regitur' is indicative
hints.Perfect passive indicative: Perfect passive would be 'rectum est' — 'regitur' is present passive

hints.rex, regis (noun): 'rex' is a noun (king) — 'regitur' is a verb form from 'regere' (to rule)
hints.reduco, reducere, reduxi, reductum: These are the parts of 'reducere' (to lead back) — a different verb
hints.rogo, rogare, rogavi, rogatum: These are the parts of 'rogare' (to ask) — not related to 'regere'

hints.to reign: 'to reign' is 'regnare' — 'regere' means to rule/direct (active governing)

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative would be 'regit' — 'regat' with -at is subjunctive
hints.Imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'regeret' — 'regat' is present subjunctive
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'reget' — 'regat' is present subjunctive in a wish

hints.regno, regnare, regnavi, regnatum: These are the parts of 'regnare' (to reign) — a different verb from 'regere'
hints.reddo, reddere, reddidi, redditum: These are the parts of 'reddere' (to give back) — not related
hints.rogo, rogare, rogavi, rogatum: These are the parts of 'rogare' (to ask) — not related to 'regere'

hints.Accusative: Accusative neuter is the same form, but 'caelum' is the subject of passive 'regitur'
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 'caelo' — 'caelum' is nominative subject
hints.Vocative: This is not addressing heaven — 'caelum' is the subject of 'regitur'

translation: O happy race of mortals, if the love by which heaven is ruled were to rule your minds.
